title_en: The Early Days of Islam in Mecca
content_en:
The first three years of Islam's spread were characterized by quiet, private invitation — a period that Islamic historians call the 'secret da'wah' (al-da'wah al-sirriyyah). The Prophet ﷺ did not make a public declaration immediately after the first revelation but instead called those closest to him in gradual stages, testing the waters and building a core community before the inevitable confrontation with the Meccan establishment.

Among the first to accept Islam were those who knew the Prophet ﷺ most intimately and were therefore best positioned to judge his character. Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, his beloved wife of fifteen years, was the first. She accepted immediately and without hesitation, a response rooted in her intimate knowledge of his honesty and nobility. Ali ibn Abi Talib, his young cousin who lived in his household, was the first among the youth to accept. Zayd ibn Haritha, his freed slave and adopted son, was among the first from this category. And Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the Prophet's closest friend and one of the most respected men in Mecca, accepted with the same speed and conviction that would characterize his entire life.

Abu Bakr's acceptance was particularly consequential because he was a highly connected and socially respected figure in Mecca whose example carried influence. Through his personal efforts in the early days of Islam, he brought into the faith several individuals who would become among the most prominent Companions: Uthman ibn Affan, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Talhah ibn Ubaydullah — all of whom would later be among the ten Companions promised Paradise by the Prophet ﷺ.

After three years of private invitation, the command of Allah came to make the call public: 'And warn your nearest kindred' (Quran 26:214). The Prophet ﷺ first invited the Banu Hashim clan to a gathering and announced his mission. When his uncle Abu Lahab dismissed him contemptuously, the Prophet ﷺ turned next to the broader public, famously standing on the hill of al-Safa and calling out to the tribes of Quraysh one by one, asking: 'If I told you that behind this hill there is an army about to attack you, would you believe me?' When they agreed that they would — for they had never experienced him as a liar — he declared: 'I am a warner to you before a severe punishment.' Abu Lahab again responded with contempt, cursing him — a response memorialized forever in the Quran's Surah al-Masad, which declares Abu Lahab's condemnation.

The Meccan leadership's initial response to the Prophet's ﷺ public call combined ridicule and pragmatic concern. The Quraysh recognized that the new message fundamentally challenged not only their religious traditions but the entire social and economic order centered on the Kaaba and its idols. The annual pilgrimage was the engine of Meccan commerce; if the idols were abolished, the Kaaba's drawing power — and with it Mecca's wealth — would be at risk. Thus what began as a theological dispute quickly became a struggle with profound economic and political dimensions.
